title: Unique Paths
code: public int UniquePaths(int m, int n)
Array.Fill(row, 1);                        // top row: one path each
row[j] += row[j - 1];              // dp[i][j] = up (old row[j]) + left (row[j-1])

dp.label: dp[i, j] = number of right/down paths from (0,0) to (i,j)

frames.caption: Base case: the entire top row and left column are 1 — only one straight path reaches each. Fill the interior top-to-bottom, left-to-right.
frames.note: Every cell in the top row and the left column has exactly ONE path into it: to reach a top-row cell you can only have walked straight right the whole way, and to reach a left-column cell you can only have walked straight down. So they are all seeded to 1 for free — these are the base cases that anchor the recurrence, and forgetting them poisons the entire table.

frames.caption: dp[1,1] = up dp[0,1]=1 + left dp[1,0]=1 = 2.
frames.note: Each interior cell can be entered from exactly two neighbors: the cell directly ABOVE (you arrived moving down) and the cell directly to the LEFT (you arrived moving right). Those sub-paths are disjoint and cover every way in, so the count is simply their sum: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. In the rolling-row code this is `row[j] += row[j-1]` — the old row[j] still holds the up value before we overwrite it, and row[j-1] already holds this row's left value.

frames.caption: dp[1,2] = up dp[0,2]=1 + left dp[1,1]=2 = 3.
frames.note: Each interior cell can be entered from exactly two neighbors: the cell directly ABOVE (you arrived moving down) and the cell directly to the LEFT (you arrived moving right). Those sub-paths are disjoint and cover every way in, so the count is simply their sum: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. In the rolling-row code this is `row[j] += row[j-1]` — the old row[j] still holds the up value before we overwrite it, and row[j-1] already holds this row's left value.

frames.caption: dp[1,3] = up dp[0,3]=1 + left dp[1,2]=3 = 4.
frames.note: Each interior cell can be entered from exactly two neighbors: the cell directly ABOVE (you arrived moving down) and the cell directly to the LEFT (you arrived moving right). Those sub-paths are disjoint and cover every way in, so the count is simply their sum: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. In the rolling-row code this is `row[j] += row[j-1]` — the old row[j] still holds the up value before we overwrite it, and row[j-1] already holds this row's left value.

frames.caption: dp[2,1] = up dp[1,1]=2 + left dp[2,0]=1 = 3.
frames.note: Each interior cell can be entered from exactly two neighbors: the cell directly ABOVE (you arrived moving down) and the cell directly to the LEFT (you arrived moving right). Those sub-paths are disjoint and cover every way in, so the count is simply their sum: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. In the rolling-row code this is `row[j] += row[j-1]` — the old row[j] still holds the up value before we overwrite it, and row[j-1] already holds this row's left value.

frames.caption: dp[2,2] = up dp[1,2]=3 + left dp[2,1]=3 = 6.
frames.note: Each interior cell can be entered from exactly two neighbors: the cell directly ABOVE (you arrived moving down) and the cell directly to the LEFT (you arrived moving right). Those sub-paths are disjoint and cover every way in, so the count is simply their sum: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. In the rolling-row code this is `row[j] += row[j-1]` — the old row[j] still holds the up value before we overwrite it, and row[j-1] already holds this row's left value.

frames.caption: dp[2,3] = up dp[1,3]=4 + left dp[2,2]=6 = 10.
frames.note: Each interior cell can be entered from exactly two neighbors: the cell directly ABOVE (you arrived moving down) and the cell directly to the LEFT (you arrived moving right). Those sub-paths are disjoint and cover every way in, so the count is simply their sum: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. In the rolling-row code this is `row[j] += row[j-1]` — the old row[j] still holds the up value before we overwrite it, and row[j-1] already holds this row's left value.

frames.caption: Answer is dp[2,3] = 10 — there are 10 unique right/down paths across the 3x4 grid.
frames.note: The bottom-right cell counts every right/down path across the whole grid. Each cell was computed once from two O(1) reads, so the table is O(m*n) time; because a row only depends on the row above plus the cell to its left, one rolling array of length n gives O(n) space. (A closed form C(m+n-2, m-1) also exists, but the DP table is the expected interview answer and generalizes to obstacle variants.)
